subject.description: Same person across both panels — white female, early 40s, professional, dark hair pulled back loosely. LEFT PANEL: relaxed posture, slight confident smile, one hand on coffee mug, looking at screen with ease — the look of someone who trusts what they're seeing. RIGHT PANEL: same woman, leaning forward with both hands on desk, pale, lips pressed tight together — the grimace of someone who just found something they can't explain. Brow furrowed, one side higher than the other. Nostrils barely flared.
subject.action: LEFT: Leaning back, reviewing dashboard casually, at ease. RIGHT: Leaning in hard, face close to the screen, one hand pressed flat on the desk.

setting.location: Modern office desk. LEFT PANEL: Clean, organized, warm morning light — everything in order, neat desk, coffee steam rising, screen showing green metrics. RIGHT PANEL: Same desk, same person, but now papers are scattered, coffee forgotten and cold, screen casting harsh light on her face.
setting.time_of_day: LEFT: Morning, golden light through windows. RIGHT: Afternoon, flat harsh overhead fluorescent.

composition.framing: Single image split down the middle vertically. Each panel is a close-up — crown with headspace at top, cut at base of neck. Eyes land in upper third of each panel. LEFT panel: cool blue-grey tones. RIGHT panel: warm harsh amber. The vertical split is a hard line.

lighting.type: LEFT: Soft flattering window light — everything looks fine. RIGHT: Harsh overhead fluorescent — unflattering, exposing, nowhere to hide.
lighting.color_grading: LEFT panel: Cool blue-grey, desaturated, clinical — the color of blind trust. RIGHT panel: Harsh warm amber-orange — the uncomfortable heat of reality hitting. The color contrast IS the entire message.

mood: LEFT: False confidence — the ease of someone who doesn't know yet. RIGHT: The specific sick feeling of discovering you've been explaining wrong numbers to clients for three weeks.
aesthetic_style: Shot on Sony A7 with 80mm lens. Split diptych composition — left panel cold and clinical, right panel warm and harsh. Skin pores visible, individual hair strands visible. Both moments feel caught, not posed. Looks like stills from a prestige documentary.
